turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-108, Snyder versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: In 2013 the City of Portage, Indiana awarded two contracts to a local truck company, Great Lakes Peterbilt to purchase trash trucks. James Snyder was the mayor of Portage. Now, like many jurisdictions around the country neither Indiana nor Portage apparently prohibited local officials from obtaining outside employment and Snyder was a contractor for Great Lakes Peterbilt, the truck company that had provided the trash trucks to Portage, and he performed, he said, consulting services for the company. In 2014 Great Lakes Peterbilt cut a $13,000 check to Snyder. Snyder said that the payment was for his consulting services, but the Federal Government charged and a federal jury convicted Snyder of accepting an illegal gratuity for the trash truck contract in violation of Section 666 of Title 18. The District Court sentenced Snyder to one year and nine months in prison. On appeal, Snyder argued that as a matter of law Section 666 criminalizes only bribes for future official acts, not gratuities for past acts. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit disagreed based on that Court’s precedent interpreting the law to cover both bribes and gratuities. So the Court of Appeals affirmed Snyder’s conviction. In light of disagreement in the Courts of Appeals about whether Section 666 covers only bribes or covers both bribes and gratuities, we granted review. We now reverse. All agree that Section 666 prohibits state and local officials from accepting bribes that are promised or given before the official act. Those bribes are punishable by up to 10 years imprisonment. The question however is whether this law also makes it a crime for state and local officials to accept gratuities, for example, gift cards, lunches, plaques, books, framed photos, or the like that may be given as a token of appreciation after the official act. Some gratuities can be problematic. Others are commonplace and might be innocuous. A family gives a holiday tip to the mail carrier, parents send an end of the year gift basket to their child’s public school teacher, a college dean gives a college sweatshirt to a city council member who comes to speak at an event. A state legislator’s neighbor drops off a gift card to congratulate her for her work on a new law. We conclude that Section 666 does not cover gratuities. There are 19 million state and local officials around the country. Some of them are part-time and have other employment. Importantly state and local governments often regulate the gifts that state and local officials may accept. So gifts to those officials are already regulated to the degree that state and local governments think appropriate. Section 666 does not supplement those state and local rules by subjecting state and local officials to up to 10 years in federal prison for accepting even commonplace gratuities. Now, we reach that conclusion for several reasons taken together, including the statutory text, the statutory history, federalism and fair notice. First in 1986 when enacting this statute, Congress modeled the text of Section 666 for state and local officials on the bribery provision for federal officials, not on the differently worded gratuities provision for federal official. The statutory text therefore strongly suggests that Section 666 is a bribery statute. Second and reinforcing that is the statutory history. In 1984 when first enacting a prior version of this statute for state and local officials, Congress did borrow language from the gratuity statute for federal officials, but just two years later in 1986, Congress overhauled the statute, eliminated the gratuities language, and instead enacted the current language that resembles the bribery provision for federal officials. We must respect Congress’ choice in 1986. Third is federalism. As I mentioned, state and local governments have made carefully calibrated policy decisions about the gifts that state and local officials may accept. Those policies would be largely overridden if we were to accept the Federal Government’s interpretation. Reading Section 666 to create a federal prohibition on gratuities would suddenly subject 19 million state and local officials to a new and different regulatory regime for gratuities. Fourth is fair notice. The government’s interpretation of the statute would create traps for unwary state and local officials. Now, the government says that the statute does not cover innocuous or obviously benign gratuities and would only cover wrongful gratuities. But the flaw in the government’s approach and it’s a very serious real world problem is that the government does not identify any remotely clear line separating an innocuous or obviously benign gratuity from a criminal gratuity. Is a $100 Dunkin’ Donuts gift card for a trash collector wrongful, a $200 Nike gift card for a county commissioner, could students take their college professor out to Chipotle for an end of term celebration? And if so, would it somehow become criminal if it’s a steak dinner or to treat her to a Hoosiers or a Fever game? The government offers no clear rules so how are state legislators, city council members, school board officials, building code inspectors, probation officers, human resource directors, police officers and the entire army of 19 million state and local officials to know what is acceptable and what is criminalized by the Federal Government. They can’t and that is a major problem with construing Section 666 to cover gratuities. In past cases when construing a statute like this, this Court has emphasized that a statute in this field that can linguistically be interpreted to be either a meat ax or a scalpel should reasonably be taken to be the ladder. So it is here. In sum, a state or local official can violate Section 666 when he accepts an upfront payment for a future official act or agrees to a future reward for a future official act. In other words, a bribe. But a state or local official does not violate this law if the official has taken the official act before any gratuity is agreed to, much less given. To be clear, a gratuity offered and accepted after the official act may be unethical or illegal under state or local laws, but the gratuity does not violate Section 666. Section 666 is a vitally important statute, but its focus is targeted. It prescribes bribes to state and local officials while allowing state and local governments to regulate gratuities to state and local officials. Congress can always change the law if it wishes to do so, but since 1986 it has not, presumably because Congress understands that state and local governments often carefully regulate gratuities to state and local officials. We reverse the judgement of the Court of Appeals. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justices Sotomayor and Kagan have joined.
